we had engineers interested in using the cli rather than any ui. obviously they can now use a genic cli so they don't need to even visit a ui because they can ask the ilm what's going on, what kind of information they need, what format they needed, etc.

we had this amazing infrastructure that was bigger and bigger. we identified that people were accessing the architecture more often than at the beginning because the architecture was growing. when we have one component or two components or five components, let's say two uis, two frontend servers running in different ports, then two or three databases, then more components like databases, backend services, and more infrastructure like infrastructure as code service and security as a service, we end up growing a lot of our infrastructure. everybody was building their own tools but nobody understood what to look at if they didn't know exactly what their environment was looking like.

we created a react flow-based graphic. when you visit that graphic, we have all the different nodes with descriptions and some information. you could open it and play around with the service. you could fork it into your own project and use it at least as the basis of the available github repo because a lot of it was confidential and a lot of it was client-based. we had almost a sandbox for everything and a running sandbox. using this, people were allowed to discover a lot of things that existed inside our ecosystem and that was amazing because we didn't need everybody to run the same sandbox, the same backend service, because everything was actually running. they look at the interface, look at the graphic. they identify what was going on behind the graphic and they could do two things:
1. identify all the modules that they already knew existed, the new services, the new tools, the new mcp servers, etc.
2. look at the issues inside those because some of those were serving actual users inside the company and some of them were just for personal use for example a product team so they were allowed to understand the status of everything.

yeah what is non-mvp? it's a really complete set of features that at a certain point in time we are not able to measure the impact. we assume or we have certain information that those features are not delivering the same value as the features and the actual core of the product we are delivering so we need to be pretty cautious about this.

in terms of things that are obvious for example:
- super polished aesthetics
- a ui that is glamorous and heavy in the web browser
- a lot of things that are obviously not part of the mvp


also things that could live in the limits of being a feature that deserves to be in an mvp and a feature that is not going to be deserving to be in an mvp. how we can decide that is the expertise of the subject matter expert. what product are we building and our stakeholders will be able to help us to identify that. it depends on the product. we will be able to understand what we are doing in terms of let's say:
- uber: we are letting people contact, ask for a ride, pay for the ride, and then go wherever they are going to


rather than a lot of features like:
- sharing your location with friends
- splitting the bill
- recording this


so it's a lot of tools that are around the most important core value.

ok so last project i conducted research on was the landing page for a product called trusted environment. this is a developer portal platform with several capabilities from terraforming infrastructure code all the way down to developer tooling.

in order to be effective we need to make sure that we identify what methodologies we use and how we're going to fit into the overall picture of the product. the marketing tells us the vision of the company because it was an internal initiative but also in the future trusted environment will be available to the public like bursa or netlify. we need to make sure that we will be available for internal usage, keeping in mind that these two will be kind of near future. this tool will become public.

user experience is key in this process. after choosing the right methodology we gather the information and processes using ai first and then learn about the insights of that information. we figure several things all the way down from pain point resolution to pricing, cost, operations, customer service, etc. and then we trust a roadmap, a plan for the landing page as a product. we then determine what will be the best business outcome in terms of metrics, what is how success looks like in terms of the varying metrics.

well when ai emerged, people started building their own small apps inside trusted environments team so they ended up implementing some things that were useful but totally absolutely totally outside the design system. in order to not be restrictive or a pain or a bottleneck in the process, i simply created some agents that will check at least at some level of compliance with their design system. it's not great to police something that is used by two or three people but it needs some standardization so in order to function it has to be based on our tokens and our components. the templates, the boilerplate, the governance, and everything around the design system will be embedded into the agents. it was embedded into the agents that i implemented.

the agent will ask:
- is this production-ready?
- is this for a client?
- what is the use case?


if the use case is internal usage then it has a framework to apply how rigorous the design system will be inside your team in your product. at least you are using some level of the design system; it feels like it's yours and it is inside your branding. all these small teams (teams to describe cyber security operations, infrastructure, and developers) just use the agents and they are automatically compliant with our design system.

I think, in an environment that is properly set up, using AI, for example, when you can export components into the same repo, you can extract the proper information for frontend engineers to use it. In a perfect world, you will connect your Figma or your cloud co-design, whatever gen AI design tool you are using, to a repo. Okay, so you connected to it, then you have exactly the same base of the engineers that are working. You implement interfaces using your experience, UI compliance, etc., and you know that the UI pages will be consistent with everything in the hands of the engineers, so they will be able to implement it with less friction.

What I'm trying to say is that the setup is the best way to have a successful handoff. You can have the most amazing and impressive documented setup in Figma. If your team doesn't need that, then your probability of being successful will be a little low. Putting everybody on the same page will be key for this, and then you can discuss what assets they need. Nobody has to have the last word. You can talk to engineers and say, "Where are you working? How does a normal prop day of work look with you? How are you implementing the science today?" I can offer you these and that. I think this is the best workflow for us. Let's brainstorm that, and then you will have success because everybody knows exactly what is going on.

Generally speaking, I would say that, in some companies, it depends on the mindset. Some people are frustrated and they just say, "Okay, tell me what to do and I'll do it." That's the last thing if you want to hear it. The other thing is, "Okay, let me participate in the design handoff process so I will get what I need, from tokens in a JSON file to Tailwind to whatever engineers need."

First, it's important to recognize that these are edge cases, and they should only be addressed after we have documented, executed, or evaluated everything outside of those edge scenarios.

As a general principle, we provide services to our customers, and our goal should be to extract and deliver the maximum value possible. Therefore, this should be considered out of scope for the MVP.

After that, we should focus on capturing as much information as possible so it can be properly evaluated later through an appropriate pipelineÃÂÃÂ¢ÃÂÃÂÃÂÃÂfrom ideation and value assessment to prioritization and implementation decisions.
